scenario: Langchainrb work session - add a new LLM provider with tool-calling support

map_quality.rationale: The answer hands the agent a complete, navigable map: identifies Langchainrb as a Ruby LLM gem, names lib/langchain/llm/ + LLM::Base, lib/langchain/assistant.rb, assistant/llm/adapters/, assistant/messages/, and llm/response/ — all the entry points needed to act with small follow-up lookups.

specificity.rationale: Paths are concrete throughout: 'lib/langchain/assistant.rb', 'lib/langchain/assistant/llm/adapters/base.rb', 'lib/langchain/assistant/messages/openai_message.rb', 'lib/langchain/llm/response/base_response.rb' — abstractions are consistently tied to actual files and class names rather than vague layers.

justification.rationale: The answer separates 'llm_clients' (per-provider client classes) from 'assistant_adapters' (format requests/parse tool calls), and steps 4-6 of the trace make the client-does-API-calls vs adapter-formats-tool-calling split clear. Could be slightly more explicit upfront about why both exist, but the why is recoverable.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer is highly confident throughout with no explicit flags about gaps or unverified assumptions in how layers connect; minor hedges ('if custom format needed', 'may need') exist in later steps but step 1 itself does not flag uncertainty about the orientation.

steps.step: Trace how the assistant talks to a provider for a tool-calling turn

map_quality.rationale: The answer traces the full path with file:line: assistant.rb:135-146 entry, chat_with_llm at 338-348 calling llm_adapter.build_chat_params, the Adapter.build factory at adapter.rb:8-24, extract_tool_call_args at 366, plus the round-trip through provider client and response. The agent could follow this exactly to wire a new provider's selection.

specificity.rationale: Dispatch pieces are named precisely with file:line: 'Adapter.build(llm) factory' at adapter.rb:8, build_chat_params at openai.rb:16-31, extract_tool_call_args at line 366, build_tools at 67-69. Not vague at all.

justification.rationale: The answer explains the role of each step (formats request, parses tool calls, dispatches to client) but does not explicitly call out WHY selection is an is_a? chain on the LLM instance to keep the Assistant provider-agnostic, nor frame Adapter.build as the single spot a new provider must teach.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer describes Adapter.build but does not flag that it is a hardcoded is_a? chain (not a registry) requiring an explicit branch, nor surface any verification caveats — it presents the trace as fully confirmed.

steps.step: Map the provider-integration family and its contract

map_quality.rationale: Enumerates the shared base (adapters/base.rb) with all five contract methods (build_chat_params, extract_tool_call_args, build_message, support_system_message?, tool_role) at specific lines, and lists every existing adapter (OpenAI, Anthropic, GoogleGemini, MistralAI, Ollama, AwsBedrockAnthropic) with file paths and how each differs. The agent can pick any adapter as a template and implement a new one with only small lookups.

specificity.rationale: Cites concrete files like `lib/langchain/assistant/llm/adapters/base.rb` with method line numbers (16, 30, 42, 49, 56) and each implementation file by path. Differences are tied to concrete details (e.g. 'tool_choice with {type:'function',function:{name:...}} format', 'disable_parallel_tool_use flag').

justification.rationale: Explains differences concretely (Anthropic passes system as top-level, Gemini uses function name as tool_call_id, AwsBedrockAnthropic inherits from Anthropic) which conveys WHY each adapter exists, though it doesn't explicitly state the meta-rationale that 'each provider has a different tool-calling wire format.' The per-adapter notes carry the why implicitly.

uncertainty.rationale: Does not flag any contract method as unconfirmed or note quirks the agent should verify before copying (e.g. whether build_tools/allowed_tool_choices are truly required vs convention). Adds 'additional_methods_from_implementations' but presents them as facts rather than uncertainties.

steps.step: Map the per-provider message and response families

map_quality.rationale: Maps both parallel hierarchies completely: message base at lib/langchain/assistant/messages/base.rb with the llm?/tool?/system?/to_hash contract, all five per-provider message classes (openai_message, anthropic_message, google_gemini_message, mistralai_message, ollama_message), the response base at lib/langchain/llm/response/base_response.rb, and all five per-provider response wrappers, with the adapter-to-message wiring shown (e.g., 'used_by_adapter: lib/langchain/assistant/llm/adapters/openai.rb:43') and the client-to-response wiring shown (e.g., 'returned_by: lib/langchain/llm/openai.rb:148').

specificity.rationale: Every message and response class is named by concrete file path (messages/openai_message.rb, llm/response/anthropic_response.rb, etc.) with line numbers for adapter wiring (:41, :42, :43, :45) and client wiring (:126, :148, :64). Contract methods are named precisely.

justification.rationale: Implicitly conveys per-provider necessity via the differences captured (e.g., 'AnthropicMessage: No system role; tool messages sent as role:user with content type:tool_result', 'GoogleGeminiMessage: LLM role is model; tool hash uses functionResponse format'), which show shapes differ. But it doesn't explicitly state the 'why' as a unifying principle, leaving the reader to infer it from the per-class notes.

uncertainty.rationale: Does not flag any provider whose message/response coverage was uncertain. Notably, AwsBedrockAnthropic appears in adapters but the answer doesn't note whether it reuses AnthropicMessage/AnthropicResponse or needs its own — exactly the kind of edge case the rubric asks to flag.

steps.step: Map the raw LLM client family and its contract

map_quality.rationale: Enumerates the base (lib/langchain/llm/base.rb with chat/complete/embed/summarize contract) and per-provider clients (openai.rb:122-148, anthropic.rb:106-126, google_gemini.rb:39-64, mistralai.rb:37-42, ollama.rb:183-311) with explicit return-line for the wrapped response, and explains the raw-JSON-to-Response flow at the end.

specificity.rationale: Concrete file:line for each client's chat method and exact response wrapping line (e.g. 'lib/langchain/llm/openai.rb:148' returning OpenAIResponse), plus the contract methods chat/complete/embed/summarize are named.

justification.rationale: The 'client_to_response_flow' note explains clients send HTTP, get raw JSON, wrap in Response so tool_calls can be extracted in native format; the client-vs-adapter separation is implicit across the answer but not crisply stated in this step's section.

uncertainty.rationale: Notes Ollama uses faraday and GoogleGemini uses Net::HTTP directly, but never flags unconfirmed capabilities like which clients lack embed() or summarize(), nor calls out anything to verify before copying a pattern.

steps.step: Find every site that must change to add a new tool-calling provider

map_quality.rationale: Enumerates the full four-way fan-out plus dispatch: new client (llm/<provider>.rb), adapter (adapters/<new_provider>.rb) with explicit Adapter.build branch at adapter.rb:8, message class (messages/<provider>_message.rb), response wrapper (response/<provider>_response.rb), and the spec matrix (client, response, adapter, message, adapter_spec). Tool_definition.rb conditional edit is a useful bonus. Agent can open the PR from this list.

specificity.rationale: Every site is named concretely with file path and often line number (e.g., 'lib/langchain/assistant/llm/adapter.rb:8', 'lib/langchain/tool_definition.rb:111-136'), plus exact contract methods (build_chat_params, extract_tool_call_args, tool_role) for each new file.

justification.rationale: Each site is tied to its hierarchy (client vs adapter vs dispatch vs message vs response vs tests) with purpose stated (e.g., 'Adapter class inheriting from Base, implementing build_chat_params...'). Could more explicitly say WHY each layer is separate, but the role of each is clear.

uncertainty.rationale: Notes flag what static reading misses: 'Rails autoloading handles discovery — no explicit require statements needed', 'gemspec may need development dependency', 'VCR cassettes may be needed'. Conditional tool_definition.rb edit ('ONLY if...not OpenAI/Anthropic/Gemini') shows calibrated uncertainty. Could flag dependency_helper gem requirement explicitly.

map_quality.rationale: The edit map enumerates all four parallel hierarchies (client, response, adapter, message) with concrete create paths, the Adapter.build dispatch edit at lib/langchain/assistant/llm/adapter.rb:8-24, the optional tool_definition.rb format change, and a full spec matrix including adapter_spec. A teammate could open the PR from this list with only small follow-up lookups.

specificity.rationale: Every file is named concretely (e.g., 'lib/langchain/llm/<new_provider>.rb', 'lib/langchain/assistant/llm/adapters/<new_provider>.rb') with file:line insertion points for the Adapter.build branch (line 8) and tool_definition.rb (111-136), not vague pointers.

justification.rationale: Each file's purpose is explained per hierarchy (client = raw API, response = wrapper, adapter = Assistant formatting, message = provider message shape), and tool_definition's conditional edit is justified ('ONLY if the new provider needs a custom tool format'). Could more explicitly tie why each hierarchy is parallel, but the contract-level reasoning is present.

uncertainty.rationale: Flags genuine uncertainty: tool_definition.rb edit is conditional on format reuse, gemspec dependency 'may need' to be added, VCR cassettes 'may be needed'. Could more sharply call out that the is_a? dispatch is hand-edited and easy to miss, but it does name it as a modify-site.
